other_observers: many including Kara Lewantowicz, Lisa Edwards, Brandon Percival, Dan Maynard, Ken Pals, etc.; Both Kara and Lisa found it at the same time
specific_location: Fountain Creek Regional Park, by the nature center building and its nearby ponds.
description: This was a dark blackish-brown large-sized raptor, longer looking when perched than a RTHA. The cere stood out because it was yellowish and contrasted with the dark coloration of the bird. When perched a broad white subterminal tail band was visible on the ventral surface of the tail. The legs appeared longer than the common CO buteos. The underwing pattern (see photo) was striking when the bird mantled after flying twice. There was much more white at the bases of the individual primaries and secondary feathers. In flight the bird was characteristic of the species. The very broad-based wings were apparent. The short tail gave the overall impression of a raptor that was uncharacteristic compared to other CO raptors. In flight ALL the flight feathers and the tail feathers were worn. (I looked in BNA online and this species in its NA range molts once per year in October which can explain why there feathers were so worn. The tail tips were thinly white; there was a broad subterminal dark band on the tail and then proximal to that there was a broad white tail band and proximal to that there was more dark on the tail at the base.Behaviors: Mostly sat up in a tall cottonwood. I saw the bird flush twice on its own and twice more when a photographer insisted it was his right to get closer. It mostly glided and did not travel more than a 100 meters each time it flew, sometimes less.Call: non heardPlumage: adult
